text: The video shows a bilateral pantaloon hernia with both direct and indirect hernias visible. On the left side, the sigmoid colon is adhered between the hernias. The bladder is visible, and the inferior epigastric vessel is located between the direct and indirect spaces.

The surgeon identifies the median umbilical ligament and observes a pantaloon hernia on the right side, with visible direct and indirect spaces. The transabdominal preperitoneal approach begins with dissection six centimeters lateral to the outer margin of the deep ring at the two o'clock position. Cold scissors are used to cut the peritoneum, creating a pre-peritoneal pocket. The triangle of doom is visible at the level of the deep ring, and the peritoneum is stretched medially and inferiorly to separate the inferior epigastric vessels.

The surgeon continues dissection six centimeters lateral to the outer margin of the deep ring at the two o'clock position, progressing towards the medial umbilical ligament. The dissection avoids going medial to the ligament, and a large 15 by 30 mesh is prepared for placement, resembling a butterfly shape. The retropubic space is accessed, with the bladder visibly separated downwards, and the dissection extends into the space of Retzius.

The surgeon identifies the pubic symphysis and the left side of the Cooper's ligament, extending dissection from the right to the left side. The lateral dissection is initiated to enlarge the pocket for the introduction of a 15 by 30 mesh. The peritoneal dissection reveals the triangle of pain, located lateral to the triangle of doom, and the spermatic vessels are visible.
The surgeon uses a grasper to pull the hernia sac inferomedially, separating it from the transversalis fascia. The peritoneum is visibly pulled as the sac is maneuvered.

The surgeon performs medial dissection of the direct hernia space, separating the transversalis fascia from the peritoneum. The Cooper ligament on the right side is visible, along with the bladder below in the retropubic space. The sac is extracted, providing additional peritoneum for mesh coverage. The pocket is enlarged around the defect to facilitate mesh placement over the myopectineal orifice.

The surgeon positions the mesh between the peritoneum and surrounding structures, identifying the spermatic vessel laterally and the vas deferens medially at the level of the deep ring. The peritoneum is separated, revealing the left side with the sigmoid colon above, situated between the direct and indirect hernia spaces. The sigmoid colon is mobilized and dropped down to create a pocket around the defect. Using scissors in the left hand, the surgeon begins dissection six centimeters lateral to the hernia defect at the ten o'clock position on the left side. The dissection progresses from medial to lateral, starting from the medial umbilical ligament, with the inferior epigastric vessel visible. The indirect space lateral to the inferior epigastric vessel is separated to accommodate the mesh.

The surgeon enlarges the pocket to accommodate a larger mesh for hernia repair. The sac is pulled out and separated on the left side, with the transversus fascia being separated from the sac. The peritoneum sac moves downward while the transversus fascia moves upward, revealing the muscle defect. The Cooper ligament, visible as a pearly white structure, is identified, and further dissection is performed between the medial umbilical ligament and the bladder. The dissection is nearly complete, with additional separation needed at the level of the ring to ensure sufficient space for the large mesh.

A large 30 by 15 centimeter mesh, cut in a butterfly shape, is prepared to cover the hernia defect. The mesh is rolled and passed between the medial umbilical ligaments. The surgeon uses a grasper from the right side to pull the mesh from left to right, ensuring it is positioned securely with the intact peritoneum providing support.

The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to cut the vicryl suture and unfolds the large mesh on both sides, ensuring it covers the entire myopectenial orifice without rotation. The mesh is spread across the left and right sides without fixation using tackers. The extra peritoneum from the sac reinforces the mesh, covering buttonholes created by the pantaloon-shaped hernia. The peritoneum helps secure the mesh in place, and five tackers are used to cover the mesh with the peritoneum.
So thank you very much for watching this video. Have a nice day.
